steps.prompt: How many finite verbs are in this sentence?

hints.two: 'est' and 'recessit': 'Benediceret' is also a finite verb (imperfect subjunctive) in the 'dum' clause
hints.one: 'recessit': 'Factum est' and 'benediceret' are also finite verb forms
hints.four: There are exactly three finite verbs: 'est', 'benediceret', and 'recessit'

steps.prompt: The infinitive of 'factum est' translates to?

hints.to make: 'To make' is 'facere' (active) — 'factum est' here is impersonal 'it happened'
hints.to carry: 'To carry' is 'ferre' — 'factum est' is from 'fio, fieri'
hints.to trust: 'To trust' is 'fidere' — 'factum est' is from 'fio, fieri'

steps.prompt: What tense and mood is 'factum est'?
steps.reveal: Et factum est = And it happened (narrative formula)

hints.present indicative: Present would be 'fit' — 'factum est' is perfect
hints.pluperfect indicative: Pluperfect would be 'factum erat' — 'factum est' is perfect
hints.perfect subjunctive: Perfect subjunctive would be 'factum sit' — 'factum est' is indicative

hints.facio, facere, feci, factum: These are the active parts of 'facio' — 'fio' is its passive
hints.fero, ferre, tuli, latum: These are the parts of 'fero' (to carry)
hints.fugio, fugere, fugi, fugitum: These are the parts of 'fugio' (to flee)

steps.reveal: dum benediceret = while he was blessing (impf. subj. in temporal clause)

hints.imperfect indicative: Imperfect indicative would be 'benedicebat' — 'benediceret' with -ret is subjunctive
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'benedicat' — 'benediceret' is imperfect
hints.pluperfect subjunctive: Pluperfect subjunctive would be 'benedixisset' — 'benediceret' is imperfect

hints.dico, dicere, dixi, dictum: These are the parts of 'dico' (to say) — 'benedicere' is bene + dicere
hints.bene, —, —, —: 'Bene' is an adverb meaning 'well' — it is not a verb
hints.maledico, maledicere, maledixi, maledict: These are the parts of 'maledico' (to curse) — the opposite of 'benedico'

steps.reveal: 'Dum' with subjunctive emphasizes the circumstantial/ongoing nature of the action

hints.'dum' always requires subjunctive: 'Dum' can take indicative (while/as long as) or subjunctive — the mood affects meaning
hints.it is an indirect question: Indirect questions use interrogative words — 'dum' is temporal
hints.it is a purpose clause: Purpose clauses use 'ut' — 'dum' here is temporal (while)

steps.reveal: recessit = he withdrew / departed (3rd sing. perf.)

hints.present indicative: Present would be 'recedit' — 'recessit' with -essit is perfect
hints.perfect subjunctive: Perfect subjunctive would be 'recesserit' — 'recessit' is indicative

hints.cedo, cedere, cessi, cessum: These are the parts of 'cedo' (to go/yield) — 'recedere' is re- + cedere
hints.recipio, recipere, recepi, receptum: These are the parts of 'recipio' (to receive/take back)
hints.redeo, redire, redii, reditum: These are the parts of 'redeo' (to go back/return)

steps.reveal: benediceret illis = he was blessing them (dat.)

hints.ablative: 'Illis' can be ablative, but 'benedicere' + dative = to bless someone
hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'illos' — 'illis' is dative after 'benedicere'

steps.reveal: ab eis = from them (abl., governed by 'ab')

hints.dative: 'Eis' can be dative, but 'ab' requires the ablative
hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'eos' — 'eis' after 'ab' is ablative
hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'eorum' — 'eis' is ablative after 'ab'

steps.prompt: What is 'Et factum est dum...' as a narrative formula?
steps.target: Hebraism (kai egeneto) — a Septuagintal narrative introduction
choices: Hebraism (kai egeneto) — a Septuagintal narrative introduction
steps.reveal: 'Et factum est' translates Greek 'kai egeneto', reflecting Hebrew 'wayehi' — a biblical narrative formula

hints.standard classical Latin idiom: Classical Latin would not use 'factum est' this way — it reflects Greek/Hebrew patterns
hints.ablative absolute construction: Ablative absolute uses ablative noun + participle — 'factum est' is a main clause
hints.passive periphrastic: Passive periphrastic uses gerundive + esse — 'factum' is a perfect participle

translation: And it happened that while he was blessing them, he withdrew from them
